title: Cosmetic Oil on Silk
stainChemistry: Cosmetic oils (face serums, body oils, massage oils) typically contain a blend of botanical oils, silicones (dimethicone, cyclomethicone), and essential oils. On silk, silicones are particularly problematic — they coat fibers with a water-repellent film that prevents standard detergents from reaching the fiber surface. Non-silicone botanical oils penetrate and oxidize like cooking oils.
whyThisWorks: Surfactant treatment breaks through the silicone barrier on silk. For pure silicone stains, isopropyl alcohol or POG provides better initial solvent action. Multiple wash cycles may be needed because silicones re-deposit from wash water. Pre-treating is essential — silicone-coated stains resist standard machine washing.
defaultAssumption: Assume stain age is unknown and possibly heat-set unless user specifies otherwise. Start conservative.

steps: 1. Apply POG (Paint Oil Grease remover) directly to the stain. Tamp gently — 5-10 min dwell time
2. If POG plateaus: apply amyl acetate for stronger solvent action — 3-5 min dwell time
3. Apply NSD (Neutral Synthetic Detergent) to emulsify remaining oil micro-droplets. Flush with cool water — 5 min dwell time
4. Apply Acetic Acid 28% (diluted 1:10) for final pH restoration — 1-2 min dwell time
warnings: Apply solvent BEFORE water — water spreads oil stains

steps: 1. Sprinkle baking soda or cornstarch on the stain to absorb surface oil — wait 10 min
2. Brush off powder with a clean cloth
3. Apply Dawn dish soap directly to the stain. Work gently into fabric — 5-10 min dwell time
4. Rinse with warm water. Repeat soap application if stain persists
5. Air dry — do NOT use dryer until stain is completely gone
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if the stain hasn't improved after 3 treatment rounds. Take to a professional dry cleaner — they have specialized solvents for oil-based stains
warnings: Always test on a hidden seam or inside hem first — silk dyes may not tolerate spotting agents.
Never add water to a fresh oil stain first — water and oil don't mix, you will spread it
Heat from dryer permanently sets oil residue

neverDo: No protease enzymes — silk IS protein; enzymes digest the fiber
No chlorine bleach — dissolves protein fibers
No alkaline detergents (pH >8) — breaks peptide bonds
No acetone — bleaches and damages fiber structure
fiberSensitivities: Protein fiber — extremely sensitive to enzymes, alkali, and heat
Dyes bleed easily — always test first

escalation.when: If cosmetic oil stain persists after 2-3 professional treatment cycles, or if the stain has been heat-set, or if home treatment has not improved the stain after 3 attempts
escalation.whatToTell: Explain that the stain is cosmetic oil on silk, how old it is, and what products were already used. This helps the professional choose the right chemistry without duplicating failed approaches

contextQuestions: Has it been exposed to heat (dryer, iron, hot water)?
Have you already tried treating it? If so, with what products?
Is the oil fresh or has it been washed/dried already?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Stain age and heat exposure are the two biggest factors. Fresh stains with no heat exposure have the highest success rate. Heat-set or aged stains may require professional treatment.

materialWarnings: ALWAYS test on a hidden seam or inside hem first — silk dyes may not tolerate spotting agents.
